refs.note: Heius's testimony at the actio prima.
The cross at the Mamertine harbour anticipates the Gavius narrative of Book 5; Cicero plants the standing image here in advance.

Q. Tadius's books recording payments at Verres's order "to the Greek painters" --- the Cibyran brothers Tlepolemus and Hiero --- are the documentary trail from Book 1's Asian-legateship plundering to Book 4's Sicilian system.
The orphan-children of Drepanum and Heius's son under M. Marcellus's guardianship recall the ward-trustee pattern of Book 1's praetorian iniquities.
Archagathus's testimony at the actio prima.
L. Piso the elder is paired with the L. Piso Frugi of Book 3 --- the lex Calpurnia repetundis author --- as the Calpurnian Frugi virtue Verres betrays.

"Of which I spoke before": back-reference to the Himera-Thermae episode in Book 2-2 (the Stesichorus statue). Both books trace the same pattern: Scipio Aemilianus restored to a Sicilian city what Carthage had taken; Verres takes it again.
Direct address to P. Servilius Vatia Isauricus and Q. Catulus, the two senior consulares on the jury, asking each to defend his own monument-inheritance against Verres's precedent.
133 BC: the year of Tiberius Gracchus's killing and the senate's resort to the Sibylline books; the foundation of Roman state recognition of Hennensian Ceres.
The set-piece comparison of the two takings of Syracuse --- by Marcellus 212 BC, who spared the temples; by Verres in peace, who stripped them. The figure runs through 115-132.
The famous Marcellus-and-Archimedes anecdote; from a wide tradition (Polybius, Livy, Plutarch).
The Verrucii in Carpinatius's records: explicit back-reference to Book 2-2's account of how Cicero's research at Syracuse uncovered Verres's name forged out of the publicans' books.
The Verria/Marcellia substitution: closing return to Book 2-2's charge that Verres abolished the Marcellian festival and replaced it with one in his own name.
